questions.question:
Aliasing in the context of programming languages refers to:
A. multiple variables having the same memory location
B. multiple variables having the same value
C. multiple variables having the same identifier
D. multiple uses of the same variable
questions.answer: A. multiple variables having the same memory location
questions.to_find: What aliasing means in the context of programming languages

understand.plain: Aliasing occurs when two or more different variable names (or pointers/references) refer to the exact same memory location. Modifying one variable automatically changes the value seen through the other. This is a common source of subtle bugs in C programs using pointers.

keywords.explain: Two distinct names/references pointing to the same memory location so a write through one is visible through the other.
keywords.example: int x = 5; int *p = &x; int *q = &x; — p and q alias x.

keywords.explain: A specific address in RAM where a variable's value is stored.
keywords.example: Variable 'a' at address 0x1004 and pointer p = &a share address 0x1004.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='360' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>*p</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='90' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>*q</text><rect x='220' y='60' width='100' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>addr 0x1004</text><text x='270' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>value = 5</text><line x1='100' y1='50' x2='220' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='100' y1='120' x2='220' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs><text x='180' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>p and q alias same location</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two pointer variables p and q both pointing to the same memory address 0x1004 which holds value 5 — illustrating aliasing.

given.aim: Identify the correct definition of aliasing in programming languages.

terms.meaning: A secondary name or reference that maps to the same storage as another name.
terms.example: int *p = &x; makes p an alias for x.

terms.meaning: A physical address in RAM holding a variable's value.
terms.connects: Aliasing is specifically about sharing the same address, not the same value or name.

terms.meaning: A variable that stores the address of another variable.
terms.example: int *p = &a; — p holds address of a.
terms.connects: Pointers are the most common mechanism that creates aliasing in C.

given.plan: Eliminate options: same value (B) is coincidence not aliasing; same identifier (C) is re-declaration/shadowing; multiple uses (D) is ordinary usage. Only (A) same memory location captures the definition of aliasing.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: B — two variables can independently hold the same value (e.g., x=5, y=5) without aliasing. C — two variables cannot have the same identifier in the same scope; that is a compile error. D — re-using one variable is not aliasing.
steps.note: None of B, C, D describe a special relationship between distinct names.

steps.title: Confirm correct definition
steps.apply: Aliasing = two distinct names refer to the same memory cell. Classic example: int x; int *p = &x; — x and *p are aliases. Any write via *p is visible via x and vice versa.
steps.note: Aliasing complicates compiler optimizations because the compiler cannot assume two named locations are independent.

solution.result: A. multiple variables having the same memory location

questions.formula_note: Definition of aliasing: two or more names bound to the same memory address.

questions.to_find: The string printed by the printf statement

understand.plain: The string 'GATE2011' is stored in array c. Pointer p points to the start of c. p[3] and p[1] give the ASCII values of 'E' and 'A'. The expression p + p[3] - p[1] advances p by (ASCII_E - ASCII_A) = 4 positions, so it points to '2011'.

keywords.explain: Adding an integer n to a character pointer moves it forward by n characters in memory.
keywords.example: If p points to 'G', then p+1 points to 'A', p+2 to 'T', etc.

keywords.explain: Each character is internally stored as an integer (its ASCII code).

understand.visual_alt: Array c = 'G','A','T','E','2','0','1','1','\0' at indices 0-8. p[1]='A'=65, p[3]='E'=69. Offset = 69-65 = 4. p+4 points to '2' (index 4), so printf prints '2011'.

given.aim: Determine what string is printed when pointer arithmetic is applied to a character array.

terms.connects: Provides ASCII values needed for the arithmetic.

terms.meaning: The character at index 3 of c, which is 'E' with ASCII value 69.
terms.connects: Used as an integer offset in pointer arithmetic.

terms.meaning: The character at index 1 of c, which is 'A' with ASCII value 65.
terms.connects: Subtracted from p[3] to compute net offset = 4.

given.plan: Compute p[3] - p[1] = 'E' - 'A' = 69 - 65 = 4. Then p + 4 points to c[4] = '2'. printf("%s", p+4) prints from index 4 to null terminator, giving '2011'.

steps.title: Find ASCII values of p[1] and p[3]
steps.note: In C, a char in an arithmetic expression is promoted to int, so p[3] - p[1] = 69 - 65 = 4.

steps.title: Compute the pointer expression
steps.apply: p points to c[0] = 'G'. Adding 4 gives a pointer to c[4] = '2'. The substring from c[4] to the null terminator is "2011".
steps.note: printf("%s", ...) prints characters from the given address until '\0', so it outputs 2011.

questions.formula_note: Pointer arithmetic: adding integer n to char* advances by n bytes; char used in arithmetic is its ASCII integer value.

questions.to_find: The three values printed by the printf statement involving 2D array pointer arithmetic

understand.plain: In C, the name of a 2D array x is a pointer to its first row. x+3 gives the address of the 4th row. *(x+3) dereferences to the first element of the 4th row — same address. *(x+2)+3 gives row 3's start (row 2 start + 2 rows) + 3 elements = same address 3036.

keywords.explain: A 2D array name decays to a pointer to its first row (pointer to array of 3 ints). Adding k moves by k * row_size bytes.
x+k is a pointer to the k-th row; *(x+k) is also the address of the first element of that row.
keywords.example: x+2 and *(x+2) both yield the address of x[2][0].

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='190'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='20' class='tag ink'>Row 0: addr 3000</text><rect x='10' y='28' width='110' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='65' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1  2  3</text><text x='10' y='75' class='tag ink'>Row 1: addr 3012</text><rect x='10' y='83' width='110' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='65' y='103' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>4  5  6</text><text x='10' y='130' class='tag ink'>Row 2: addr 3024</text><rect x='10' y='138' width='110' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='65' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>7  8  9</text><text x='10' y='185' class='tag ink'>Row 3: addr 3036</text><rect x='10' y='168' width='110' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='65' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>10  11  12</text><text x='200' y='40' class='lbl ink'>x+3   = 3036</text><text x='200' y='80' class='lbl ink'>*(x+3)= 3036</text><text x='200' y='120' class='lbl ink'>*(x+2)+3 = 3024+12 = 3036</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 4x3 int array starting at 3000. Each row occupies 3*4=12 bytes. Row 0 at 3000, Row 1 at 3012, Row 2 at 3024, Row 3 at 3036. x+3 and *(x+3) both give 3036. *(x+2)+3 = 3024 + 3*4 = 3036.

given.aim: Compute the three pointer expressions for a 4x3 int array whose base address is 3000.

terms.meaning: x[0][0] is at address 3000; each int is 4 bytes.
Each row has 3 ints * 4 bytes = 12 bytes.
terms.connects: Used to compute x+3 and x+2.

terms.meaning: Dereferencing a row pointer gives the address of the first element of that row, which equals the row's address.

given.plan: Compute each expression: x+3 = 3000+3*12 = 3036; *(x+3) = address of x[3][0] = 3036; *(x+2)+3 = address of x[2][0] + 3*sizeof(int) = 3024+12 = 3036.

steps.title: Compute x+3
steps.note: x is a pointer to int[3], so each step advances by 12 bytes.

steps.title: Compute *(x+3)
steps.apply: *(x+3) dereferences the row pointer at row 3; this yields the address of x[3][0] = 3036. Same numeric value as x+3 when printed as unsigned int.
steps.note: *(x+3) is of type int*, pointing to x[3][0]; its value as an address is 3036.

steps.title: Compute *(x+2)+3
steps.note: Adding 3 to an int* advances by 3*sizeof(int) = 12 bytes.

questions.formula_note: 2D array pointer arithmetic: array name decays to pointer-to-row; adding k rows advances k * num_cols * sizeof(element) bytes.

questions.question:
Consider the following two C code segments. Y and X are one and two dimensional arrays of size n and n x n respectively, where 2 <= n <= 10. Assume that in both code segments, elements of Y are initialized to 0 and each element X[i][j] is initialized to i+j. Further assume that when stored in main memory all elements of X are in the same memory page frame.

Code segment 1:
for (i=0; i<n; i++)
    Y[i] += X[0][i];

Code segment 2:
for (i=0; i<n; i++)
    Y[i] += X[i][0];

Which of the following statements is/are correct?
S1: Final contents of array Y will be identical in both code segments.
S2: Elements of X accessed inside the loop shown in code segment 1 are contiguous in main memory.
S3: Elements of X accessed inside the loop shown in code segment 2 are contiguous in main memory.

A. Only S2 is correct
B. Only S3 is correct
C. Only S1 and S2 are correct
D. Only S1 and S3 are correct
questions.answer: C. Only S1 and S2 are correct
questions.to_find: Which statements about the two code segments are correct regarding output and memory access patterns

understand.plain: Both code segments compute a partial sum of matrix X into array Y. Segment 1 accesses row 0 across columns (X[0][0], X[0][1], ...) which are contiguous in memory (row-major). Segment 2 accesses column 0 down rows (X[0][0], X[1][0], ...) which are NOT contiguous since each row is n ints apart. The final values of Y differ between the two segments because X[0][i] = 0+i = i whereas X[i][0] = i+0 = i — they are actually equal! So S1 is also correct.

keywords.explain: C stores 2D arrays row by row in memory: X[0][0], X[0][1], ..., X[0][n-1], X[1][0], ...
Elements are contiguous if they occupy consecutive memory addresses.
keywords.example: X[0][0] to X[0][n-1] are contiguous; X[0][0] and X[1][0] are n*4 bytes apart.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe y='55' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1</text><rect x='100' y='55' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='75' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>4</text><rect x='60' y='85' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2</text><rect x='100' y='85' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3</text><rect x='140' y='85' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>4</text><rect x='180' y='85' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>5</text><rect x='60' y='115' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3</text><rect x='100' y='115' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>4</text><rect x='140' y='115' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>5</text><rect x='180' y='115' width='40' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>6</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ok'>Seg1: row 0 (highlighted) — contiguous</text><text x='10' y='175' class='sub no'>Seg2: col 0 — NOT contiguous (stride=n)</text><text x='280' y='80' class='sub ink'>Y[i] same</text><text x='280' y='100' class='sub ink'>in both:</text><text x='280' y='120' class='sub ink'>X[0][i]=i</text><text x='280' y='140' class='sub ink'>X[i][0]=i</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 4x4 matrix X[i][j]=i+j. Code segment 1 accesses row 0: X[0][0..3]={0,1,2,3} (contiguous). Code segment 2 accesses col 0: X[0..3][0]={0,1,2,3} (not contiguous, stride=n). Both produce the same Y since X[0][i]=i and X[i][0]=i.

given.aim: Determine which statements about correctness of output and memory access patterns of two C code segments are true.

terms.example: X[0][3]=3, X[3][0]=3 — symmetric about the anti-diagonal.
terms.connects: Makes X[0][i] = i and X[i][0] = i, so both segments add 0..n-1 into Y, giving identical Y.

terms.meaning: C stores 2D arrays row by row: X[0][0],X[0][1],...,X[0][n-1],X[1][0],...
terms.example: X[0][0] and X[0][1] are adjacent; X[0][0] and X[1][0] are n ints apart.
terms.connects: Determines whether accesses in each segment are contiguous.

terms.meaning: Successive loop iterations access adjacent memory locations.
terms.example: X[0][i] for i=0,1,2,... accesses consecutive addresses — good for cache.
terms.connects: S2 is true (seg 1 accesses row 0 = contiguous); S3 is false (seg 2 accesses col 0 = stride n).

given.plan: Check S1: compute Y from both segments. Check S2: seg 1 access pattern in memory. Check S3: seg 2 access pattern in memory.

steps.title: Verify S1: Are final Y arrays identical?
steps.apply:
Segment 1: Y[i] += X[0][i] = 0+i = i. So Y[i] = i.
Segment 2: Y[i] += X[i][0] = i+0 = i. So Y[i] = i.
Both produce Y = {0, 1, 2, ..., n-1}. S1 is CORRECT.
steps.note: The symmetry X[0][i] = X[i][0] = i makes both sums identical.

steps.title: Verify S2: Segment 1 memory access pattern
steps.apply: Segment 1 accesses X[0][0], X[0][1], ..., X[0][n-1]. In row-major order these are consecutive memory locations (same row). So they ARE contiguous. S2 is CORRECT.
steps.note: Consecutive array elements in the same row are adjacent in memory.

steps.title: Verify S3: Segment 2 memory access pattern
steps.apply: Segment 2 accesses X[0][0], X[1][0], X[2][0], ..., X[n-1][0]. In row-major order X[i][0] and X[i+1][0] are n ints = 4n bytes apart. These are NOT contiguous. S3 is INCORRECT.
steps.note: Column access in row-major storage always has stride = n, causing poor cache utilization.

solution.result: C. Only S1 and S2 are correct

questions.formula_note: Row-major 2D array storage in C: X[i][j] stored at base + (i*num_cols + j)*sizeof(element); same-row elements are contiguous, same-column elements have stride = num_cols.
